policy_statement: A12: Support on-site rainwater retention and utilization (and greywater use) through integrating such systems into public investments and creating regulatory instruments and support schemes for private investments; require/encourage delayed runoff, retention or reuse for major emitters (e.g., industry, malls, office blocks) (continuous).
mechanism_description: It develops regulatory instruments and support schemes to integrate on-site rainwater retention, delayed runoff, and reuse (including greywater use), reducing stormwater runoff and improving water resilience.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (heavier rainfall/drought) → ↓ runoff peak / ↑ retained water → ↓ flooding & overflow exposure + ↑ water-supply resilience
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Buildings and sites (public and private, including large “emitters” of runoff like malls/industry) are required or incentivized to capture, detain, infiltrate, and/or reuse rainwater and sometimes greywater via tanks, cisterns, green roofs, permeable surfaces, and reuse plumbing.  
2) Mediating physical process: Increased on-site storage and delayed discharge reduce peak flow rates to drainage networks; infiltration and reuse reduce effective runoff volumes and substitute for potable supply, increasing buffering during dry periods.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower exposure to pluvial flooding and combined sewer overflows during extreme rainfall and improved water security/resilience during droughts and heat-related water demand spikes.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced flood/combined-sewer overflow risk (and improved water-supply reliability) by attenuating stormwater peaks and increasing local water availability under climate-stressed conditions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: manages rainfall at/near source to reduce flood/overflow impacts and increase available water during dry periods—an exposure/sensitivity reduction pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While there can be secondary mitigation/resource-efficiency co-benefits (reduced potable pumping/treatment energy), the central mechanism described is runoff reduction and “water resilience,” which is adaptation-focused.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a program is framed mainly to reduce energy use or emissions from water supply systems, it could lean toward Resource Efficiency/Mitigation; however, this canonical mechanism explicitly targets stormwater/runoff and resilience, keeping Adaptation as primary.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy centered on on-site retention/delayed runoff/reuse principally reduces vulnerability to extreme precipitation and water stress, regardless of city context.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment is deferred: Any mitigation/resource-efficiency co-benefits should be captured at the individual policy level based on evidence (e.g., quantified energy savings), not at this mechanism-level primary classification.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some implementations emphasize potable-water substitution (reuse/greywater) and could be argued as Resource Efficiency if the dominant stated objective and measured outcome is reducing freshwater withdrawals/energy use; nevertheless, for this mechanism as defined (runoff reduction + resilience), Adaptation remains primary.
key_indicators: “regulatory instruments”; “support system”; “major emitters”; “compelled to apply”; “delayed runoff, retention or secondary use of rainwater”; “utilization and retention of rainwater”
co_benefits: Reduced potable water demand and utility costs; improved urban amenity/greening potential; potential water-quality benefits from reduced combined sewer overflows.
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes both mandates (“compelled”) and enabling finance (“support system”); classified by the most forceful/central lever in the excerpt (regulatory requirement), with incentives treated as complementary.
